text.title: Fine Tune Vs Prompt Economic Model Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every application of Fine Tune Vs Prompt Economic Model must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Inputs to the model are explicitly named with their unit, range, and source-of-truth. Inputs without units are rejected.

rationale.source: Quantitative-modelling discipline (Doug Hubbard, How To Measure Anything, 2010)

rule.rationale: Quantitative-modelling discipline (Doug Hubbard, How To Measure Anything, 2010).

rule.statement: Sensitivity ranges are computed for the top 3 inputs. A model with no sensitivity analysis is a single-point estimate dressed up.

rationale.source: Monte-Carlo / scenario analysis practice from finance

rule.statement: Model is re-run when any named input moves outside its declared range. Stale model outputs are tagged 'expired'.
rule.rationale: yesterday's model on today's reality is malpractice.

example.title: Anchored application
example.code: Trigger: Fine-tune vs prompt-engineer decision flow (4 weeks worst case) Artefact: structured output meeting the contract in content/02-output-contract.xml Evidence: ≥1 named source per rule (URL, ticket, transcript_id) Decision: downstream consumer can act without re-deriving reasoning
example.note: Demonstrates the closed loop the methodology creates — trigger → artefact → decision — with auditability at each step.

example.title: Un-anchored application
example.code: Trigger: 'we should do this thing' Artefact: prose paragraph, no schema Evidence: 'industry best practice' Decision: ambiguous — every reader interprets differently
example.note: Each ambiguity above is a failure mode catalogued in content/03-failure-modes.xml. The methodology exists to prevent exactly this shape.
